comments.text: Hi both, just a heads-up that ChatGPT (free version) just one-shotted this for me. The proof was barely over a page long, and it looks pretty legit. I'm assuming this probably means there's an existing solution somewhere in the literature, but I have been unable to find it. I have no idea how to use Lean or how to contribute this to Mr Tao's GitHub, and Latex wasn't rendering properly for me in this comment box, so I'm just going to give you guys a link to my PDF of the proof. I have enabled viewing and commentary for everyone.

The proof of minimality seems incomplete to me. What if $a_k+1$ is prime (as apparently is the case for $k=15$ not to mention $k=1,2$)? It seems that $n_k$ is in fact maximal among $n$ with $\phi(n)=a_k$.

This proof is wrong - it says that the smallest $n$ such that\[\phi(n) = \prod_{i=1}^k (p_i-1)\]is $n=\prod_{i=1}^kp_i$. This fails already for $k=2$ - for example $\phi(3)=\phi(2\cdot 3)$. (There is an obvious problem in that one can just divide by $2$ without changing the value of $\phi(n)$. But even excluding this there are problems, such as\[\phi(3\cdot 5\cdot 7) = \phi(5\cdot 13)\]and $13<3\cdot 7$.)

As a third voice, I'll simply confirm that the proof is wrong in step 1: minimality, like the other two comments. You can read ChatGPT's reasoning here, which I haven't checked but aligns closely with the other comments. I also note that this looks like it's generated by GPT 5.2 Instant from stylistic considerations, but I won't claim this as fact. So one advice is to explicitly at least use GPT-5.2 Thinking for mathematical problems. Another advice is to ask it to check the proof itself - GPT-5.2 Thinking can catch a nontrivial fraction of its own errors this way.

I have reported this negative result in Section 1 of the wiki. It is of course more exciting to report the positive results, but I believe it is also important to report negative results to try to have as complete a picture as possible of what these tools are currently capable of, and what they are not, and what workflows and model versions perform better at mathematical tasks than others.
FYI I would personally be highly sceptical of the mathematical output from GPT if you’re not using the Pro model. Not that it doesn’t also make mistakes, but they are often a lot more subtle.

Most likely the answer is negative, i.e., if $n$ is minimal amongst all numbers with a given value of $\phi(n)$, then $n/\phi(n)$ must be bounded (or equivalently, $\sum_{p|n} 1/p = O(1)$). Intuitively, if $n$ has too many prime factors, it should be possible to swap out some prime factors for others to reduce $n$ while maintaining exactly the same value of $\phi(n)$. But it looks very difficult to prove this rigorously with our current analytic number theory technology!

This is a good heuristic. Just a minor refinement: such an argument should prevent n from having many *small* prime divisors. We cannot expect to prevent n from having many prime factors of any size. Namely, if there were infinitelly many Fermat primes, then their products (like $n=3*5*17*257$) would be good examples of such n. Luckily, such large primes do not contribute much to the sum of reciprocals, of course.

Also, the possibility of such swapping procedure is related to questions like #203, where one would use such prime p to replace some powers of 2 and 3 from $a$. But already these questions seem to be too difficult.
